text.title: User Interviews Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every user-interviews application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Research questions (what we need to learn) MUST be written BEFORE interview questions (what we will ask). 5-10 research questions per study.
rule.rationale: Skipping the upstream layer produces interview guides that drift from the actual goal.

rule.statement: Interview questions MUST be open-ended AND ask about past behaviour, NOT hypothetical future behaviour.
rule.rationale: Hypotheticals invite confabulation; past behaviour is verifiable from artefacts.

rule.statement: Session notes MUST separate observation ("what users said") from inference ("what we infer"). Inference without source quote is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Conflating the two bakes in early interpretations and prevents re-analysis.

rule.statement: Interviewer words ≤20% of transcript. Long monologues from the interviewer indicate a leading session.
rule.rationale: High interviewer share signals coaching or leading; quote ratio is the auditable signal.

rule.statement: Interviewer MUST NOT react positively or negatively. Reactions train participants to repeat the rewarded behaviour.
rule.rationale: Praise contaminates the data; neutral acknowledgement is mandatory.

rule.statement: Record sessions with explicit consent. Notes alone are insufficient; agent synthesis is bounded by what was captured.
rule.rationale: Without recording, paraphrase becomes the upper bound of synthesis depth.

rule.statement: Every insight statement MUST cite at least one verbatim quote (with participant id) from the sanitised transcripts. No quote, no insight.
rule.rationale: Quote-citation makes insights reviewable and reproducible.

rule.statement: Transcripts MUST be sanitised (participant names → IDs, PII removed) BEFORE passing to external LLM APIs.
rule.rationale: Raw transcripts contain PII; default-on sanitisation is the lowest-friction protection.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
